modules.title: Faith First
modules.subtitle: Anchor to something bigger than the grind

sections.title: Why we start here
sections.body:
Before goals, before grades, before the grind — there is you, and there is what you believe in.

Faith doesn't have to mean religion. Your anchor can be a higher power, your culture, your family, the people you carry, a mission, or your own deepest values. What matters is that it exists. Discipline without a center collapses under pressure. With a center, pressure becomes fuel.

This module is about naming your anchor and committing to checking in with it. We start here because everything else in this app — every module, every habit — pulls from this well.

sections.title: Name your anchor
sections.prompt: What is the one thing — bigger than school, bigger than career — that you want to live in alignment with? It can be God, your faith tradition, your people, a personal mission, or the kind of human you want to be. Just name it. Be specific.

sections.title: How will you check in
sections.prompt: How will you check in with your anchor day to day? Prayer, journaling, meditation, a phone call home, a verse, a quote, a moment of silence — what works for you?

modules.title: Know Your Why
modules.subtitle: Define the motivation behind the work

sections.title: The cost of working without a why
sections.body:
Most students burn out not because the work is too hard but because they forgot why they signed up. Your why is the answer you give yourself at 2 a.m. when you'd rather quit. If it's borrowed from your parents, your friends, or a LinkedIn post — it won't hold.

A real why is specific, personal, and a little embarrassing to say out loud. That's how you know it's yours.

sections.title: The five whys
sections.prompt: Write down what you're working toward right now. Then ask 'why?' five times in a row, each answer building on the last. The fifth answer is your real why.

questions.prompt: A strong personal 'why' is most useful when:
Which of these is a sign your 'why' is borrowed, not yours?
options: You can defend it to a stranger
You learned it from a podcast yesterday

questions.prompt: Why does the module recommend five 'whys' instead of one?
options: It surfaces the real motivation under surface answers

modules.title: Ball Is Life
modules.subtitle: Every sport teaches the same lesson

sections.title: Life is a game — play it that way
sections.body:
Whether your sport was basketball, football, soccer, track, swimming, dance, esports, or none — every sport teaches the same lesson. You are accountable for your assignment whether or not anyone is watching. You box out when the ball is across the court. You finish the rep when the coach turned around. You make the cut you're afraid to make.

Here's the bigger frame: life is a temporary game. You have one body, one window, one limited number of possessions. Nobody plays forever. The people you admire treat life the way great athletes treat their sport — with respect, urgency, and craft. They study film on themselves. They practice when nobody's clapping. They show up for every play.

That is the lens. Treat your days like possessions. Treat your goals like reps.

sections.title: Your assignment
sections.prompt: What is one 'assignment' in your life right now that no one is checking on, but that you know matters? It could be a class, a project, a person, your health, your faith. Name it — and decide how you'll show up for it this week like a pro.

sections.title: What if isn't a feeling — it's a model
sections.body:
Most people treat 'what if' as anxiety. Smart people use it as a risk model.

Here's the question: if the worst outcome of doing this thing isn't worse than the default of doing nothing, you go. Because the upside is asymmetric — your downside is capped at 'where you already are,' and your upside is uncapped.

Applied to college life: what if you send the cold email? Worst case, they don't reply. That's the same as not sending it. What if you apply to the program everyone says you won't get into? Worst case, you don't get in. That's the same as not applying. What if you start the thing? Worst case, it doesn't work. That's the same as not starting.

But what if it works? What if it exceeds your expectations? That asymmetry is the entire game.

sections.title: Run the model
sections.prompt: Pick one thing you've been hesitating on. Write: (1) What if it goes badly — what's the realistic worst case? (2) Is that actually worse than not doing it at all? (3) What if it exceeds your expectations — what does that look like?

sections.title: Three what-ifs
sections.prompt: Write three 'what if' statements about your life that you've never said out loud. They should feel slightly uncomfortable to type.

modules.title: LinkedIn
modules.subtitle: Build your professional presence as a student

sections.title: Presence is a career multiplier
sections.body:
If you are a student trying to start a serious career — internships, full-time roles, research, your own company — your professional presence is doing work for you 24/7, whether you're paying attention to it or not.

Recruiters search for you before they meet you. Alumni decide whether to reply to your DM based on what they see. The difference between an opportunity and a dead end is sometimes just whether your profile looks like someone worth backing.

This isn't vanity. This is leverage. A strong presence opens doors that grades and resumes alone won't open. LinkedIn is the cheapest, fastest tool for building one — but you have to actually use it like a tool, not a social network.

sections.title: The three-line headline
sections.body:
A great student headline does three things in under 220 characters: (1) names what you are, (2) names what you're building toward, (3) gives one specific signal that proves you're serious.

Example: 'CS @ Howard | Building consumer apps with real users | Prev: SWE intern @ Capital One'.

Specific beats clever every time. 'Aspiring' is the most expensive word on the platform — drop it. You either are or you're working on it. Both are stronger than aspiring.

sections.title: Update your headline now
sections.body: Open your LinkedIn profile and rewrite your headline in the three-line format. Come back and confirm when done.

sections.title: The only ones who show up every day
sections.body:
Friends will come and go. Teammates graduate. Family gets busy. The internet's attention is rented. The only person who will be there for you every day of your life is you — and whatever you anchored to in module one.

Discipline is what that relationship looks like in practice. It is the rep you do for yourself when nobody's clapping. The class you read for even though attendance isn't taken. The workout you finish because you told yourself you would.

This is the truth nobody wants to hear: motivation is a guest. Discipline is the resident. You're not training to feel like doing the thing. You're training to do the thing whether you feel like it or not.

sections.title: Pick your one
sections.prompt: What is ONE habit you will commit to every day for the next 30 days? Write it as a goal you can check off in this app. Make it small enough that skipping feels worse than doing.

sections.title: The cost of the mask
sections.body:
Performing a version of yourself you think people want is exhausting and, eventually, illegible. People connect with specificity, not polish. The story of where you're actually from, what you actually love, what you've actually struggled with — that is your edge.

In a feed full of curated wins, the realest profile wins.

sections.title: What you usually edit out
sections.prompt: What is one true thing about your story that you usually edit out when you introduce yourself? Why?

sections.title: Discipline is a team sport
sections.body:
The lone wolf myth is a marketing campaign. Every person you admire has a group chat. They have people they show their work to before it's ready, people who notice when they go quiet, people who hold the line on standards when motivation drops.

Join one community in LOCKD In this week. Make your goals public to that group. Discipline you outsource to a community is discipline that survives.

sections.title: Join a community
sections.body: Browse student communities and join at least one. Make at least one of your goals public so the community can see your check-ins.

sections.title: Perfection is a direction, not a destination
sections.body:
If you treat perfection as a place you arrive at, you'll never arrive. If you treat it as a direction you point yourself in every day, it becomes the most powerful compounding force in your life.

The math is wild. 1% better every day for a year is roughly 37x. Not 37% — 37 times. You will not feel the difference on day 12. You will not feel it on day 50. You will feel it on day 200 when you look back at the person who started.

This isn't perfectionism. Perfectionism freezes you because you're scared of the judgment of others. The 1% rule is the opposite — you ship, you note what was below your standard, and you raise it tomorrow. The standard moves, and you move with it.

sections.title: The 1% you'll add today
sections.prompt: What is one small thing in your current work — a project, an application, a profile, a habit — that you can make 1% better today? Not perfect. Just better.

sections.title: From why to what
sections.body:
Your 'why' is your motivation. Your purpose is the shape your life takes because of it. A purpose statement is one sentence you can recite that tells you, for the next ten years, what to say yes to and what to say no to.

This is the last module. After you finish it, you are LOCKD In.

sections.title: Your one-sentence purpose
sections.prompt: Draft a single sentence: 'My purpose is to ___ so that ___.' Don't worry about getting it perfect — you'll edit it for years. Just write the first real version.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a 'why' and a 'purpose' in this curriculum?
options: Why is motivation; purpose is the shape it gives your life
Why is for school; purpose is for work

questions.prompt: What are you supposed to do with your purpose statement after writing it?
options: Use it to decide what to say yes and no to
